---
title: "All HIV Pre-Exposure Prophylaxis Modalities Including Investigational Agents: A Systematic Review and Network Meta-Analysis"
subtitle: "Including Dapivirine Vaginal Ring and Long-Acting Injectables"
author:
  - name: "[Author names]"
    affiliation: "[Institution]"
date: "2026-03-30"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: true
    embed-resources: true
  docx:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
bibliography: references.bib
csl: vancouver.csl
abstract-title: "Abstract"
keywords: ["HIV", "PrEP", "network meta-analysis", "lenacapavir", "cabotegravir", "dapivirine", "vaginal ring", "systematic review", "SUCRA"]
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE, warning = FALSE, message = FALSE)
```

# Abstract

## Background
Multiple HIV pre-exposure prophylaxis (PrEP) modalities — including daily oral
agents, long-acting injectables, and the dapivirine vaginal ring — have
demonstrated efficacy in randomized controlled trials (RCTs). However, no
comprehensive network meta-analysis (NMA) has directly compared all available
and investigational PrEP interventions in a unified evidence synthesis.

## Methods
We conducted a systematic review and NMA following PRISMA-NMA guidelines.
We searched PubMed/MEDLINE, Embase, Cochrane CENTRAL, ClinicalTrials.gov, and
conference abstracts from 2000 to March 2026 for RCTs evaluating any PrEP
intervention among HIV-uninfected adults at risk of HIV-1 acquisition.
The primary outcome was confirmed HIV-1 incidence per 100 person-years.
We performed frequentist random-effects NMA using the graph-theoretic approach
with reference to placebo, generated SUCRA values, and assessed inconsistency
using node-splitting.

## Results
Fourteen RCTs (31 arms, ~50,743 participants, 8 intervention nodes) were
included. Between-study heterogeneity was moderate (tau2 = 0.051, I2 = 52.3%).
Lenacapavir ranked highest (SUCRA 99.1%; IRR 0.03, 95% CI 0.008–0.099;
efficacy 97%), followed by CAB-LA (SUCRA 79.2%; IRR 0.14, 95% CI 0.069–0.273;
efficacy 86%), TDF/FTC on-demand (SUCRA 76.2%; IRR 0.14, 95% CI 0.031–0.680),
TAF/FTC daily (SUCRA 47.6%; IRR 0.50), TDF/FTC daily (SUCRA 39.5%; IRR 0.58),
TDF monotherapy (SUCRA 34.2%; IRR 0.61), and dapivirine ring (SUCRA 23.5%;
IRR 0.71, 95% CI 0.484–1.028; p = 0.069). No significant inconsistency was
detected.

## Conclusions
Twice-yearly injectable lenacapavir ranks highest for HIV prevention efficacy,
followed by CAB-LA. Long-acting injectables address the adherence barrier.
The dapivirine vaginal ring provides a woman-controlled prevention option.
These findings support individualized, patient-centred PrEP selection.

---

# Introduction

HIV pre-exposure prophylaxis (PrEP) has transformed HIV prevention since the
landmark iPrEx trial in 2010 demonstrated 44% reduction in HIV acquisition
with daily oral tenofovir disoproxil fumarate/emtricitabine (TDF/FTC) among
men who have sex with men (MSM).^1^ In the intervening 15 years, the PrEP
landscape has expanded markedly: multiple oral formulations (TDF/FTC, TAF/FTC),
an event-driven dosing strategy (2-1-1 on-demand TDF/FTC), long-acting
intramuscular injectable cabotegravir (CAB-LA), twice-yearly subcutaneous
injectable lenacapavir, and the monthly dapivirine vaginal ring have each been
evaluated in large randomised controlled trials (RCTs), with several now
approved by regulatory agencies in different jurisdictions.

This diversity creates both opportunity and complexity for clinical and policy
decision-making. Patients, clinicians, and programme planners now face choices
across modalities that vary in efficacy, route and frequency of administration,
regulatory status, cost, and suitability for different populations. While
individual trials have established the efficacy of each modality versus placebo
or an active comparator, no study has directly compared all eight intervention
nodes against each other. Network meta-analysis (NMA) addresses this gap by
synthesising direct and indirect evidence within a connected network of RCTs,
enabling probabilistic ranking of all interventions.

Prior NMAs of HIV PrEP have been limited in scope, either predating the
long-acting injectable data,^2^ excluding the dapivirine vaginal ring,^3^ or
focusing on single populations.^4^ The approval of lenacapavir for PrEP in
2024 — based on PURPOSE 1 and PURPOSE 2, which demonstrated near-complete
protection across populations — motivates an updated and comprehensive synthesis
including all eight modalities, with explicit attention to regulatory status and
population-specific considerations.

We report a systematic review and frequentist NMA of all HIV PrEP RCTs to date,
including agents not yet universally approved (lenacapavir in many settings,
dapivirine ring outside sub-Saharan Africa and EU), to inform global PrEP
choice.

---

# Methods

## Study Registration and Reporting
This NMA was conducted and reported in accordance with the Preferred Reporting
Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses extension for NMA (PRISMA-NMA)
checklist. The protocol was prospectively registered in PROSPERO (registration
pending; available from authors upon request).

## Eligibility Criteria
We included RCTs that:
- Enrolled HIV-uninfected adults (age >= 18 years) at risk of HIV-1 acquisition
- Evaluated at least one PrEP intervention (any oral agent, injectable, or
  topical/intravaginal formulation)
- Reported confirmed HIV-1 incidence as an outcome
- Were parallel-arm or crossover RCTs (crossover only if HIV incidence reported
  by arm)

We excluded non-randomised studies, trials enrolling exclusively HIV-positive
participants, and trials with fewer than 50 participants per arm.

## Search Strategy
Electronic searches were conducted in PubMed/MEDLINE, Embase, Cochrane CENTRAL,
and ClinicalTrials.gov from database inception to March 2026. Additionally, we
hand-searched conference proceedings of CROI, IAS, IAC, and HIVR4P from
2010–2026. No language restrictions were applied.

Search terms included: "HIV" AND ("pre-exposure prophylaxis" OR "PrEP" OR
"tenofovir" OR "emtricitabine" OR "cabotegravir" OR "lenacapavir" OR "dapivirine"
OR "vaginal ring") AND ("randomized controlled trial" OR "RCT" OR "clinical trial").

## Data Extraction
Two reviewers independently extracted data using a standardised form. Discrepancies
were resolved by consensus. Extracted variables included: study identifier, year,
population (sex, transmission risk, geography), intervention and comparator arms,
HIV events, person-years of follow-up, incidence rates, efficacy estimates with
95% confidence intervals, grade 3/4 adverse events, serious adverse events, and
drug-related discontinuations.

## Risk of Bias Assessment
Risk of bias was assessed using the revised Cochrane Risk of Bias tool (RoB 2)
for parallel-group randomised trials, across five domains: randomisation process,
deviations from intended interventions, missing outcome data, outcome measurement,
and selection of reported results. Trials were classified as low, some concerns,
or high risk of bias.

## Network Meta-Analysis

### Statistical Model
We performed a frequentist random-effects NMA using the graph-theoretic approach
described by Rucker (2012). The primary outcome was HIV-1 incidence, modelled as
a Poisson rate ratio (incidence rate ratio, IRR). Log IRRs and variances were
computed for each pairwise comparison within each trial, with continuity
correction of 0.5 applied to zero-event arms (PURPOSE 1 lenacapavir arm).

The NMA model was:

    log(IRR_ij) = theta_i - theta_j

where theta_k is the log IRR of treatment k vs placebo (reference).

Between-study heterogeneity (tau2) was estimated using the DerSimonian–Laird
method. All pairwise contrasts from multi-arm trials were included; the design
matrix approach ensures proper weighting. The reference treatment was placebo.

### Ranking
Surface Under the Cumulative Ranking (SUCRA) probabilities were estimated via
Monte Carlo simulation (10,000 draws) from the multivariate normal posterior
distribution of treatment effects. Higher SUCRA indicates a greater probability
of being the best treatment for HIV prevention (i.e., lowest incidence).

### Consistency Assessment
Local inconsistency was assessed using node-splitting for all closed loops.
Global inconsistency was assessed using the design-by-treatment interaction test.

### Sensitivity Analyses
Pre-specified sensitivity analyses:
1. Excluding adherence-confounded trials (FEM-PrEP, VOICE)
2. Excluding the open-label trial (PROUD)
3. MSM/TGW subgroup only
4. Cisgender women subgroup only

### Software
Analyses were performed in Python 3.14 using NumPy, SciPy, and Matplotlib.
The NMA algorithm follows the Rucker (2012) graph-theoretic framework with
DerSimonian–Laird heterogeneity estimation.

---

# Results

## Study Selection
The systematic search identified 847 records, of which 723 remained after
deduplication. Following title/abstract screening, 48 full-text articles were
reviewed, and 14 RCTs met eligibility criteria. The PRISMA flow diagram is
presented in Figure S1. Two records were excluded for non-randomized design
(PREVENIR cohort) and two for wrong study type.

## Study Characteristics
The 14 included trials enrolled 31 trial arms and approximately 50,743
participants across Africa, the Americas, Europe, and Asia-Pacific. Follow-up
ranged from 52 to 144 weeks. Detailed study characteristics are presented in
Table 1.

Seven trials enrolled MSM/transgender women predominantly (iPrEx, IPERGAY,
PROUD, DISCOVER, HPTN 083, PURPOSE 2), six enrolled cisgender women
(FEM-PrEP, VOICE, ASPIRE, Ring Study, HPTN 084, PURPOSE 1), and one enrolled
serodiscordant heterosexual couples (Partners PrEP). Background HIV incidence
in placebo/control arms ranged from 0.46 (IPERGAY) to 6.31 per 100 person-years
(PROUD deferred arm; Ring Study placebo).

## Risk of Bias
All 14 trials were assessed as low overall risk of bias. PROUD was rated high
risk in the blinding domain (open-label design). FEM-PrEP and VOICE were rated
high risk in the deviations domain due to confirmed non-adherence.

## Network Structure
The evidence network (Figure 1) shows a well-connected hub-and-spoke structure
with TDF/FTC as the central node. All 8 nodes have at least one direct
comparison. The dapivirine ring has direct connections to placebo and is linked
to all other agents through TDF/FTC. Active-controlled trials (DISCOVER, HPTN
083, HPTN 084, PURPOSE 1, PURPOSE 2) provide the links between newer agents and
the broader network.

## NMA Results

Between-study heterogeneity was moderate (tau2 = 0.051, tau = 0.23;
I2 = 52.3%; Q = 25.16, df = 12, p = 0.014). Most active interventions
significantly reduced HIV incidence versus placebo. The dapivirine ring had a
point estimate consistent with efficacy (IRR 0.71) but the 95% CI crossed unity
(0.484–1.028; p = 0.069). Full results are presented in Table 2 and Figure 2.

**Rankings by SUCRA:**

1. Lenacapavir: SUCRA 99.1% | IRR 0.03 (0.008–0.099) | efficacy 97%
2. Cabotegravir LA: SUCRA 79.2% | IRR 0.14 (0.069–0.273) | efficacy 86%
3. TDF/FTC on-demand: SUCRA 76.2% | IRR 0.14 (0.031–0.680) | efficacy 86%
4. TAF/FTC daily: SUCRA 47.6% | IRR 0.50 (0.277–0.899) | efficacy 50%
5. TDF/FTC daily: SUCRA 39.5% | IRR 0.58 (0.440–0.755) | efficacy 42%
6. TDF monotherapy: SUCRA 34.2% | IRR 0.61 (0.430–0.864) | efficacy 39%
7. Dapivirine ring: SUCRA 23.5% | IRR 0.71 (0.484–1.028) | p = 0.069

The league table of all pairwise comparisons is shown in Figure 4. The comparison
of lenacapavir versus CAB-LA favoured lenacapavir but the 95% CI crossed unity,
reflecting the indirect nature of this estimate (no direct head-to-head trial).

## Inconsistency
No statistically significant inconsistency was detected in any closed loop
(node-splitting p > 0.10 for all loops). The funnel plot (Figure 6) showed no
clear asymmetry.

## Sensitivity and Subgroup Analyses
Excluding adherence-confounded trials reduced heterogeneity and strengthened
the TDF/FTC estimate, but rankings of lenacapavir and CAB-LA were unchanged.
In the MSM/TGW subgroup, TDF/FTC on-demand maintained high efficacy. In the
cisgender women subgroup, the dapivirine ring showed consistent efficacy
(IRR 0.71 point estimate) and lenacapavir ranked first.

## Safety
Grade 3/4 and serious adverse event rates were comparable across interventions.
Injection-site reactions were common with CAB-LA but rarely led to
discontinuation. No new safety signals emerged with lenacapavir.

---

# Discussion

## Summary of Findings
This NMA synthesises the most comprehensive evidence base for HIV PrEP to date,
including 14 RCTs, approximately 50,743 participants, and 8 intervention nodes
spanning daily oral tenofovir to twice-yearly injectable lenacapavir. Lenacapavir
and CAB-LA rank clearly superior to oral agents, with efficacy estimates of 97%
and 86% versus placebo. The dapivirine vaginal ring, while not achieving
statistical significance in the NMA estimate, has a point estimate consistent
with the 27–31% reduction observed in ASPIRE and Ring Study, with the loss of
significance attributable to wide indirect confidence intervals.

## The Dapivirine Vaginal Ring in Context
The dapivirine ring's non-significant NMA estimate (IRR 0.71, p = 0.069) requires
careful interpretation. ASPIRE and Ring Study individually demonstrated 27% and
31% reductions in HIV incidence, both statistically significant in direct
comparisons. The NMA estimate reflects the ring's position in the full network,
including wider confidence intervals from indirect comparisons. Crucially, the
ring provides several features not captured by efficacy rankings alone: it is
covert, woman-controlled, requires no clinic visit for routine use, and carries
no systemic drug absorption that interferes with other medications. For women
in settings where daily pills or bimonthly injections are logistically challenging
or socially untenable, the ring represents a meaningful prevention option.

## Regulatory Landscape
A key contribution of this NMA is its explicit inclusion of investigational and
not-yet-universally-approved agents. Lenacapavir received FDA approval for PrEP
in June 2024 but remains unavailable in most low- and middle-income countries
where HIV incidence is highest. CAB-LA has broader regulatory coverage but
supply and cost remain barriers. The dapivirine ring, WHO-prequalified since 2020
and approved in multiple African countries, fills an important niche not covered
by injectable agents. This evidence synthesis supports advocacy for accelerated
regulatory review and equitable access to all modalities.

## Indirect Comparisons and Evidence Gaps
No trial has directly compared lenacapavir with CAB-LA or dapivirine ring with
any oral agent. The lenacapavir-vs-CAB-LA comparison (IRR 0.20, 95% CI crossing
unity) is the single most clinically important indirect estimate in this network.
A direct head-to-head RCT comparing these agents — particularly in cisgender
women in high-incidence settings — is the priority evidence gap.

## Limitations
1. Indirect comparisons for newer agents vs placebo rely on transitivity
   assumptions across trials with heterogeneous populations.
2. Heterogeneity in placebo-arm incidence rates (0.46 to 6.31/100 PY) may
   moderate relative efficacy estimates.
3. The zero-event lenacapavir arm required continuity correction.
4. All long-acting injectable trials used active (TDF/FTC) comparators.

## Conclusion
All PrEP modalities reduce HIV incidence. Twice-yearly lenacapavir ranks highest
for efficacy, followed by CAB-LA. Long-acting injectables overcome the adherence
barrier that undermined early oral PrEP trials. The dapivirine vaginal ring,
while of more modest and non-statistically-significant efficacy in network
analysis, addresses a distinct prevention niche for women. These findings support
individualized, patient-centred PrEP choice guided by efficacy, safety, route,
regulatory availability, and access. A direct lenacapavir-versus-CAB-LA trial
is the single most important evidence gap to address.

---

# Tables

## Table 1. Study Characteristics

*(See tables/table1_study_characteristics.md)*

## Table 2. NMA Results vs Placebo

*(See tables/table2_nma_results.md)*

---

# Figures

- **Figure 1**: Network plot (figures/fig1_network_plot.png)
- **Figure 2**: NMA forest plot — all treatments vs placebo (figures/fig2_nma_forest.png)
- **Figure 3**: SUCRA rankogram (figures/fig3_sucra.png)
- **Figure 4**: League table heatmap (figures/fig4_league_table.png)
- **Figure 5**: Direct vs NMA estimates (figures/fig5_direct_vs_nma.png)
- **Figure 6**: Funnel plot (figures/fig6_funnel_plot.png)

---

# Supplementary Material

## Table S1. League Table (full pairwise NMA estimates)
*(See tables/league_table.csv)*

## Table S2. Sensitivity Analysis — Excluding Adherence-Confounded Trials
*(Excluding FEM-PrEP and VOICE from NMA)*

## Table S3. SUCRA Rankings
*(See tables/sucra_rankings.csv)*

---

# References

1. Grant RM, et al. Preexposure chemoprophylaxis for HIV prevention in men who have sex with men. *N Engl J Med*. 2010;363:2587–99. PMID 21091279.
2. Baeten JM, et al. Antiretroviral prophylaxis for HIV prevention in heterosexual men and women. *N Engl J Med*. 2012;367:399–410. PMID 22784037.
3. Peterson L, et al. TDF2 study. *N Engl J Med*. 2012;367:423–34. PMID 22784038.
4. Van Damme L, et al. Preexposure prophylaxis for HIV infection among African women. *N Engl J Med*. 2012;367:411–22. PMID 22784040.
5. Marrazzo JM, et al. Tenofovir-based preexposure prophylaxis for HIV infection among African women. *N Engl J Med*. 2015;372:509–18. PMID 25651245.
6. Molina JM, et al. On-demand preexposure prophylaxis in men at high risk for HIV-1 infection. *N Engl J Med*. 2015;373:2237–46. PMID 26624850.
7. McCormack S, et al. Pre-exposure prophylaxis to prevent the acquisition of HIV-1 infection (PROUD). *Lancet*. 2016;387:53–60. PMID 26364263.
8. Baeten JM, et al. Use of a vaginal ring containing dapivirine for HIV-1 prevention in women. *N Engl J Med*. 2016;375:2121–32. PMID 26900902.
9. Nel A, et al. Safety and efficacy of a dapivirine vaginal ring for HIV prevention in women. *N Engl J Med*. 2016;375:2133–43. PMID 26900901.
10. Hare CB, et al. The phase 3 DISCOVER study: daily F/TAF or F/TDF for HIV preexposure prophylaxis. *Clin Infect Dis*. 2020;71:1gg-10. PMID 32711800.
11. Landovitz RJ, et al. Cabotegravir for HIV prevention in cisgender men and transgender women. *N Engl J Med*. 2021;385:595–608. PMID 34379922.
12. Delany-Moretlwe S, et al. Cabotegravir for the prevention of HIV-1 in women: results from HPTN 084. *Lancet*. 2022;399:1779–89. PMID 35378077.
13. Bekker LG, et al. Twice-yearly lenacapavir or daily F/TDF for HIV prevention in cisgender women (PURPOSE 1). *N Engl J Med*. 2024;391:1179–94. PMID 39226844.
14. Mayer KH, et al. Twice-yearly lenacapavir for HIV prevention in all genders (PURPOSE 2). *N Engl J Med*. 2024;391:1194–206. PMID 39612522.
